questions.prompt: In an NMOS NOR gate, the driver transistors are connected in:
questions.explanation: NOR uses parallel drivers. If ANY input is HIGH, the corresponding driver turns ON and pulls the output LOW. Only when ALL inputs are LOW does the output stay HIGH. Parallel = NOR, Series = NAND.

questions.prompt: In an NMOS NAND gate, the driver transistors are connected in:
questions.explanation: NAND uses series drivers. ALL drivers must be ON (all inputs HIGH) to create a complete path to ground and pull the output LOW. If any driver is OFF, the series chain is broken and the output stays HIGH.

questions.prompt: For a 2-input NOR gate with inputs A=1, B=0, the output Y is:
questions.explanation: NOR: Y = NOT(A OR B). With A=1, the OR result is 1, so Y = NOT(1) = 0. In circuit terms, driver T1 (controlled by A=1) turns ON, pulling the output LOW regardless of B.

questions.prompt: For a 2-input NAND gate with inputs A=1, B=0, the output Y is:
questions.explanation: NAND: Y = NOT(A AND B). With B=0, the AND result is 0, so Y = NOT(0) = 1. In circuit terms, T2 (controlled by B=0) is OFF, breaking the series chain. The load pulls the output HIGH.

questions.prompt: Why is the NOR gate preferred over NAND in NMOS logic?
options: Parallel drivers have lower pull-down resistance (faster switching)
NAND gates don't work in NMOS
questions.correctAnswer: Parallel drivers have lower pull-down resistance (faster switching)
questions.explanation: In NOR (parallel), each driver provides its own path to ground: R_total = R_on/n. In NAND (series), resistances stack: R_total = n × R_on. Lower resistance means faster pull-down and better output LOW level.

questions.prompt: What logic function does a 2-input NMOS gate with parallel drivers implement?
questions.explanation: Parallel drivers create the OR function (any one can pull down). The load transistor provides the NOT (inversion). Together: NOT(A OR B) = NOR. The key rule: parallel = NOR, series = NAND.

questions.prompt: In a 2-input NMOS NOR gate, how many input combinations produce a LOW output?
questions.explanation: NOR truth table: output is LOW for A,B = (0,1), (1,0), (1,1) — three combinations. Output is HIGH only for (0,0). This also means the gate has static power dissipation in 3 out of 4 cases.

questions.prompt: The body effect in series-connected NMOS transistors:
options: Increases the effective threshold voltage of upper transistors
Decreases switching speed of NOR gates
questions.correctAnswer: Increases the effective threshold voltage of upper transistors
questions.explanation: In series configuration (NAND), the source of the upper transistor is above ground potential. This body effect increases its effective VT, requiring higher gate voltage to turn it ON and further degrading the pull-down speed.

questions.prompt: A 3-input NMOS NOR gate has how many driver transistors?
questions.explanation: An n-input NOR gate has n parallel driver transistors (one per input) plus 1 load transistor. So a 3-input NOR has 3 drivers in parallel + 1 load = 4 total transistors.

questions.prompt: Why are NAND and NOR called 'universal gates'?
options: They can operate at any voltage
Any logic function can be built using only NAND or only NOR gates
They work in all temperature ranges
questions.correctAnswer: Any logic function can be built using only NAND or only NOR gates
questions.explanation: NAND and NOR are universal because any Boolean function (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, etc.) can be implemented using only NAND gates or only NOR gates. This is proven in Boolean algebra and is the reason these gates are the building blocks of all digital systems.

questions.prompt: In which input combinations does an NMOS NAND gate have static power dissipation?
questions.explanation: Static power flows VDD→load→drivers→GND only when the output is LOW, which requires both drivers ON (A=1, B=1). In the other 3 combinations, at least one driver is OFF, breaking the current path. NAND has lower average static power than NOR.

questions.prompt: The Boolean expression for a 2-input NOR gate is:
questions.explanation: NOR = NOT-OR. First compute the OR of the inputs (A + B), then invert. Y = NOT(A + B) = A NOR B. The bar notation is: Y = (A+B) with a bar over it.
